To view the text of this section after the expiration of all emergency and temporary legislation, click this link: Permanent Version.*  (a) When a person detains possession of real property without right, or after his right to possession has ceased, the Superior Court of the District of Columbia, on complaint under oath verified by the person aggrieved by the detention, or by his agent or attorney having knowledge of the facts, may issue a summons in English and Spanish to the party complained of to appear and show cause why judgment should not be given against him for the restitution of possession. (b) The person aggrieved shall not file a complaint seeking restitution of possession pursuant to this section for nonpayment of rent in an amount less than $600. Nothing in this subsection shall prevent the person aggrieved from filing a complaint to recover the amount owed. (c)(1) Prior to January 1, 2022, the person aggrieved shall not file a complaint seeking relief pursuant to this section, except when: (A) The complaint alleges that the tenant's continuing presence at the housing accommodation where the tenant resides presents a current and substantial threat to the health and safety of tenants, on-site agents, or employees of the owners of the housing accommodation, or household members or guests of other tenants, or residents of immediately adjacent properties, because the tenant has violated an obligation of tenancy by engaging in an unlawful possession of a firearm, threats or acts of violence, or assault; (B) The complaint alleges that the tenant has willfully or wantonly caused significant damage to the unit, building, premises, or property of the housing provider; or (C) The complaint alleges non-payment of rent, the complaint is filed on or after October 12, 2021, and the person aggrieved provides documentation at the time of filing demonstrating that: (i) He or she has applied for emergency rental assistance through the STAY DC program on behalf of the tenant, or initiated the application on behalf of the tenant by completing all landlord portions of the application, the tenant has been notified in writing of the application, and the housing provider is eligible to seek possession pursuant to § 42-3505.01(b-1)(1); and (ii) The tenant has been served with a written notice which meets the requirements of § 42-3505.01(b-1)(2) and all other requirements under District law. (2) It shall be a dispositive affirmative defense requiring dismissal of a complaint for non-payment of rent if a tenant can demonstrate with substantial evidence provided through testimony that: (A) The housing provider did not pursue rental assistance as required timely or in good faith; (B) The tenant did not receive notice of the rental assistance application; (C) The housing provider did not provide a notice that meets the requirements of [§] 42-3505.01(b-1)(2), and all other requirements under District of Columbia law; (D) The tenant or their authorized representative submitted an application for emergency rental assistance prior to or during the 60 days after receiving a past due rent notice, and that application is still pending, approved and awaiting payment, or under appeal; (E) The housing provider has not met the requirements of § 42-3505.01(b-1)(1); or (F) For complaints that involve rent arrears accrued since March 11, 2020, the landlord did not offer or negotiate a payment plan in good faith pursuant to § 42-3281 at any time since March 11, 2020. (3) For complaints filed pursuant to (c)(1)(B) of this subsection, it shall be a dispositive affirmative defense requiring dismissal of a complaint if a tenant can demonstrate with substantial evidence that the housing provider willfully or negligently contributed to the significant damage of the unit, premises, building, or property that are the subject of the complaint. (4) For purposes of this subsection, the term: (A) "Act of violence" shall have the same meaning as "crime of violence" as provided in § 23-1331(4). (B) "Assault" shall be construed according to § 22-404. (C) "Significant damage" includes large holes in the walls of the unit that cannot be repaired with plaster and paint, destruction of major building systems such as electric or plumbing, destruction of appliances such as ovens, refrigerators or dish washing machines in the unit, or damage to large areas of flooring such that the housing provider will have to replace the damaged flooring. (D) "Threat" shall be construed according to § 22-407. (E) "Unlawful possession of a firearm" shall be construed according to § 22-4503. (5) Nothing in this section shall be construed to create an obligation on the part of any person to pursue an eviction action under this subsection. (6) No tenant shall be evicted from a rental unit based on a complaint filed under this subsection unless the court determines by a preponderance of the evidence that the alleged violation of an obligation of tenancy meets all of the requirements of this subsection. (7) At the initial hearing for any complaint for non-payment of rent, if the complaint does not allege sufficient facts or the person aggrieved has not produced sufficient documentation to meet all pre-filing requirements under District law, the Court shall dismiss the complaint. (d)(1) The person aggrieved shall not file a complaint seeking restitution of possession pursuant to this section without a valid registration or claim or exemption issued pursuant to § 42-3502.05, and a current license for rental housing issued pursuant to § 47-2828(c)(1) presented at the time of filing. (2) The Court may waive the requirements in this subsection if the person aggrieved can demonstrate that the housing provider for the housing accommodation was unable to obtain or renew a current rental housing license due to extenuating circumstances. (e) The person aggrieved shall not file a complaint pursuant seeking relief to this section based on consistent late payment of rent by a tenant occurring between the dates of March 11, 2020, and 60 days after the expiration of the public health emergency declared in response to the novel 2019 coronavirus (SARS CoV-2). (f) Complaints seeking relief pursuant to this section that are not permitted to be filed pursuant to subsection (c) of this section shall not be filed until January 1, 2022, at the earliest. label: hqa_Vermont_3410
subject: Vermont
question: When a landlord wants to sell a property occupied by a tenant, does the law require they provide local government with notice?
final_answer: Answer: No
snap_answer:
To answer this question, we need to consider the specific requirements of Vermont housing law regarding the sale of a property occupied by a tenant. 

1. **Understanding Vermont Housing Law**: Vermont has specific laws and regulations governing landlord-tenant relationships, including requirements for notice in various situations such as eviction, rent increases, and potentially the sale of a property.

2. **Notice Requirements**: In many jurisdictions, including Vermont, there are laws that require landlords to provide tenants with notice in certain situations. However, the question specifically asks about notice to the local government, not the tenant.

3. **Local Government Notification**: The requirement to notify local government upon the sale of a property is not universally applied across all situations in housing law. It's more common for laws to require notification to tenants or for specific regulatory compliance (like lead paint disclosure) rather than a blanket requirement to notify local government of a sale.

4. **Vermont Specifics**: Vermont law does have provisions for landlord-tenant relationships, including notice periods for terminating a tenancy. However, the specific requirement to notify local government when selling a property occupied by a tenant is not a standard or widely recognized practice in Vermont housing law.

Given these considerations, the answer to whether Vermont law requires a landlord to provide local government with notice when selling a property occupied by a tenant would generally be no, as the primary notice requirements are typically directed towards the tenants themselves or related to specific regulatory compliance issues rather than a general obligation to inform local government of a property sale.

Answer: No
hyde_passage: Pursuant to Vermont law, specifically 9 V.S.A. § 4451, there is no explicit requirement for a landlord to notify local government upon the sale of a property occupied by a tenant, as the primary focus of notice requirements is on tenant protections and regulatory compliance. The Vermont statutes primarily address landlord-tenant relationships, including notice periods for terminating a tenancy, as outlined in 9 V.S.A. § 4467, but do not impose a general obligation to inform local government of a property sale. Under Vermont law, the responsibility to notify relevant parties in the context of a property sale is generally directed towards the tenants themselves, in accordance with the provisions of 9 V.S.A. § 4456.

Selling or renting State property|(a) As agent of the State, with the advice and consent of the Governor unless otherwise provided, the Commissioner of Buildings and General Services may lease for a term not exceeding 10 years any real property owned by the State and not used for State purposes. This subsection shall not apply to leases of land pursuant to 10 V.S.A. chapter 83.(b) Upon authorization by the General Assembly, which may be granted by resolution, and with the advice and consent of the Governor, the Commissioner of Buildings and General Services may sell real estate owned by the State. Such property shall be sold to the highest bidder therefor at public auction or upon sealed bids in the discretion of the Commissioner of Buildings and General Services, who may reject any or all bids. Notice of the sale or a request for sealed bids shall be posted in at least three public places in the town where the property is located and also published three times in a newspaper having a known circulation in the town, the last publication to be not less than 10 days before the date of sale or opening of the bids. Failing to consummate a sale under the method prescribed in this section, the Commissioner of Buildings and General Services is authorized to list the sale of this property with a real estate agent licensed by the State of Vermont. This subsection shall not apply to the sale, conveyance, exchange, or lease of lands or interests in lands; to the amendment of deeds, leases, and easements; or to sales of timber made in accordance with the provisions of 10 V.S.A. chapter 155 or the provisions of 10 V.S.A. chapter 83.(c) The provisions of subsections (a) and (b) of this section shall not be construed to allow the Commissioner of Buildings and General Services to grant oil and gas leases on State-owned lands located within Vermont, but, with the approval of the Governor, the Commissioner of Buildings and General Services may sell or lease State lands for which an oil and gas lease has been granted, subject, however, to the terms and conditions of such oil and gas lease; Service; form|(a)(1) In all cases, unless service is waived under subsection 4966(g) of this title, a copy of the notice of sale shall be served on the mortgagor or his or her representative in interest by:   (A) sending the notice by registered or certified mail addressed to the mortgagor or such representative at his or her last known address, or to such person and address as may be agreed upon in said mortgage, at least 60 days before said sale; or (B) serving the notice in any manner authorized by the Vermont Rules of Civil Procedure.  (2) As used in this section, "mortgagor" shall mean the mortgagor or the then record owner of the mortgaged property.   (b)(1) A copy of the notice of sale shall be sent to any tenant lawfully occupying the mortgaged property and to any person having a recorded interest in the mortgaged property of record which will be foreclosed by the sale, provided that the interest is recorded in the applicable land records prior to the recording of the notice of sale. The notice required by this subsection shall be sent not less than 60 days before the sale. Any mortgagor or junior lienholder who refuses to accept or claim mailed or served notice or who frustrates attempts by the mortgagee to give notice of the sale by failing to give or leave a forwarding address or by other acts or omissions shall be deemed to be notified of the sale, provided that such mortgagee shall have made a good faith effort to provide such notice. A notice of sale shall be sufficient if it fully sets forth the date, time, and place of sale; the town, county, street, or highway and street number, if any, of the mortgaged premises; the date of the mortgage; the volume and page of the recording of the mortgage; the terms of the sale; the statement language required by subdivision (d)(1) of this section; and notice of the right to redeem.(d)(1) The notice of the sale shall include the following statement: "The mortgagor and all junior lienholders are hereby notified that at any time before the foreclosure sale, the mortgagor and all junior lienholders have a right to petition the Civil Division of the Superior Court for the county in which the mortgaged property is situated, with service upon the mortgagee, and upon such bond as the court may require, to enjoin the scheduled foreclosure sale. Failure to institute such petition and complete service upon the foreclosing party, or the party's agent, conducting the sale prior to sale shall thereafter bar any action or right of action of the mortgagor or any junior lienholder based on the validity of the foreclosure, the right of the mortgagee to conduct the foreclosure sale, or compliance by the mortgagee with the notice requirements and other conditions of this section. An action to recover damages resulting from the sale of the mortgaged property on the date of the sale may be commenced at any time within one year following the date of the sale, but not thereafter." Section 35-8A-412Conversion buildings. (a) A declarant of a condominium containing conversion buildings, and any person in the business of selling real estate for his or her own account who intends to offer units in such a condominium shall give each of the residential tenants and any residential subtenant in possession of a portion of a conversion building notice of the conversion no later than 60 days before the tenants and any subtenant in possession are required to vacate. The notice must set forth generally the rights of tenants and subtenants under this section and shall be hand-delivered to the unit or mailed by prepaid United States mail to the tenant and subtenant at the address of the unit or any other mailing address provided by a tenant. No tenant or subtenant may be required to vacate upon less than 60 days' notice, except by reason of nonpayment of rent, waste, or conduct that disturbs other tenants' peaceful enjoyment of the premises, and the terms of the tenancy may not be altered during that period. Failure to give notice as required by this section is a defense to an action for possession. (b) If a notice of conversion specifies a date by which a unit or proposed unit must be vacated, and otherwise complies with the provisions of Chapter 9A, the notice also constitutes a notice to terminate specified by that statute. (c) Nothing in this section permits termination of a lease by a declarant in violation of the terms of the lease. (Acts 1990, No. 90-551, p. 858, §4-112; Act 2018-403, §1.)

label: hqa_Virginia_5954
subject: Virginia
question: Secondary methods of service are defined as those methods that may be used if the primary method is unsuccessful. Are publication and certified mail permitted secondary methods of service for an eviction action?
final_answer:
To answer this question, let's analyze the provided sources step by step, focusing on the methods of service for eviction actions and whether publication and certified mail are permitted as secondary methods.

1. **Understanding Primary and Secondary Methods of Service**: The primary method of service typically involves direct delivery of the summons or eviction notice to the defendant (tenant) by a process server, sheriff, or other authorized individual. Secondary methods come into play when the primary method cannot be accomplished due to the tenant's absence, refusal to accept service, or inability to locate the tenant.

2. **Analyzing the Sources**:
   - **Source 1** discusses service of process on unincorporated associations, orders, or common carriers with their principal office outside Virginia. It mentions service on officers, trustees, directors, staff members, or agents, or on the clerk of the State Corporation Commission, but does not directly address eviction actions or secondary methods of service for such actions.
   - **Source 2 and Source 5** both refer to ex parte motions for service in cases where a tenant cannot be served with an order of eviction or writ of possession. They allow for service by affixing a certified copy of the order in a conspicuous place on the premises. While these sources indicate an alternative method of service, they specifically address situations where the tenant cannot be served directly and do not explicitly categorize these methods as secondary.
   - **Source 3** provides detailed procedures for serving summons in eviction actions, including mailing by ordinary and certified mail, and service by posting on the premises if other methods fail. It outlines various scenarios under which service is considered complete, including when certified mail has not been returned (except if refused or unclaimed) and when ordinary mail has not been returned.
   - **Source 4** mentions that the court may order service by publication if service cannot be made by another prescribed method with due diligence, in actions described in section 314 (not directly provided in the sources).

3. **Determining Secondary Methods of Service**:
   - From **Source 3**, it's clear that certified mail is a method of service that can be used, potentially as a secondary method if direct service (primary method) is unsuccessful. The source details the process for mailing by certified mail and the conditions under which service by mail is considered complete.
   - **Source 4** explicitly states that service by publication is authorized if service cannot be made by another prescribed method, implying it can be used as a secondary method when primary methods fail.

4. **Conclusion**:
   Based on the analysis, both publication (as per **Source 4**) and certified mail (as detailed in **Source 3**) are indeed permitted as secondary methods of service for eviction actions in Virginia, especially when the primary methods of service are unsuccessful.

Answer: Yes
snap_answer:
To determine if publication and certified mail are permitted secondary methods of service for an eviction action in Virginia, we need to consider the relevant statutes and rules governing service of process in eviction cases.

1. **Understanding Primary and Secondary Methods of Service**: In legal proceedings, including eviction actions, service of process is crucial. The primary method typically involves personal service or service at the defendant's usual abode. If these primary methods are unsuccessful, secondary methods may be employed.

2. **Virginia Law on Service of Process**: Virginia law outlines specific procedures for serving process in eviction cases. According to the Virginia Code, particularly § 8.01-296 and § 8.01-327, and relevant case law, the methods for serving process can include personal service, service at the defendant's usual abode, and other specified methods.

3. **Secondary Methods**: For cases where primary service methods are unsuccessful, Virginia law allows for alternative or secondary methods. These can include posting at the premises and mailing by certified or registered mail, among others.

4. **Publication and Certified Mail**: Specifically, regarding publication and certified mail as secondary methods of service:
   - **Publication** is indeed a method that can be used under certain circumstances when other methods of service have been unsuccessful. This typically involves publishing the notice in a newspaper.
   - **Certified Mail** is also recognized as a valid method for serving process in certain situations, including when personal service or service at the defendant's usual abode cannot be achieved.

5. **Eviction Actions**: In the context of eviction actions, Virginia law permits the use of these alternative methods when primary methods have failed, ensuring that the defendant is adequately notified of the proceedings against them.

Considering these points, it's clear that Virginia law does provide for the use of publication and certified mail as secondary methods of service in eviction actions, under specific conditions and when primary methods of service are unsuccessful.

Answer: Yes
hyde_passage: Pursuant to § 8.01-296 and § 8.01-327 of the Virginia Code, secondary methods of service in eviction actions may include posting at the premises and mailing by certified or registered mail, and in certain circumstances, publication. Specifically, Virginia law allows for service by certified mail when personal service cannot be made, and publication may be used as a last resort when other methods have been unsuccessful. Therefore, publication and certified mail are indeed permitted secondary methods of service for an eviction action in Virginia, under the conditions outlined in the relevant statutes.

label: hqa_Indiana_1120
subject: Indiana
question: Can a landlord evict a tenant if the property is made uninhabitable?
final_answer:
To answer this question, we need to analyze the provided passages and understand the context of Indiana housing law. 

First, let's consider the scenario where a property is made uninhabitable. According to the passages, if a landlord fails to maintain a dwelling unit in a habitable condition, the tenant has several options, including terminating the rental agreement, recovering actual damages, or applying to the court for relief (Source 1). However, the passages do not explicitly state that a landlord can evict a tenant if the property is made uninhabitable.

In fact, Source 1 suggests that if a landlord fails to remedy a material failure to maintain the dwelling unit in a habitable condition, the tenant may terminate the rental agreement immediately. This implies that the tenant has the right to terminate the agreement, not the landlord.

Additionally, Source 2 states that if the condition alleged to breach the warranty of habitability is the result of the action or inaction of a tenant, the landlord may have a defense to the tenant's claim. However, it does not provide a clear answer to the question of whether a landlord can evict a tenant if the property is made uninhabitable.

Source 3 discusses the disclosure requirements for landlords, while Source 4 deals with the termination of leases for non-dwelling purposes. Source 5 simply states that the residential landlord-tenant statutes apply to rental agreements for dwelling units located in Indiana.

Based on the analysis of the provided passages, it appears that a landlord cannot evict a tenant solely because the property is made uninhabitable. In fact, the landlord may be liable for failing to maintain the property in a habitable condition.

Answer: No
snap_answer:
To answer this question, we need to consider the Indiana housing laws regarding eviction and the obligations of landlords to maintain habitable properties. 

1. **Landlord's Obligations**: In Indiana, as in many other states, landlords have a legal obligation to ensure that their rental properties are habitable and meet certain basic standards of safety and health. This includes maintaining the structural integrity of the building, ensuring adequate water, heat, and electricity, and keeping the property free from hazards.

2. **Tenant's Rights**: Tenants have the right to a habitable living environment. If a landlord fails to maintain the property, making it uninhabitable, the tenant may have several options, including withholding rent, making necessary repairs and deducting the cost from the rent, or seeking legal action against the landlord.

3. **Eviction**: The question of whether a landlord can evict a tenant if the property is made uninhabitable is complex. Generally, a landlord cannot evict a tenant solely because the property has become uninhabitable, especially if the condition is a result of the landlord's failure to maintain the property. However, if the tenant is withholding rent due to the uninhabitable conditions, the landlord might attempt to evict the tenant for non-payment of rent.

4. **Legal Process**: For a landlord to legally evict a tenant in Indiana, they must follow the statutory eviction process, which typically involves serving the tenant with a notice to quit (if the tenant has failed to pay rent or has otherwise breached the lease) and then filing an eviction lawsuit if the tenant does not comply with the notice.

Considering these points, while a landlord might attempt to evict a tenant for various reasons, including non-payment of rent that results from disputes over the property's condition, the landlord cannot directly evict a tenant simply because the property is uninhabitable, especially if the landlord is responsible for the condition. However, the landlord might still pursue eviction through other means, such as for non-payment of rent, which could be a consequence of the property's condition.

Answer: Yes
hyde_passage: Pursuant to Indiana Code § 32-31-2-5, a landlord is required to maintain the rental property in a habitable condition, and failure to do so may constitute a breach of the landlord's obligations. However, Indiana Code § 32-31-2-6 allows a landlord to terminate a rental agreement if the tenant fails to pay rent, which may be a consequence of the tenant withholding rent due to uninhabitable conditions. According to Indiana Code § 32-31-2-9, a landlord must follow the statutory eviction process, which involves serving the tenant with a notice to quit before filing an eviction lawsuit.

If a failure is remediable and the landlord adequately remedies the failure or uses his or her best efforts to remedy the failure within 14 days after receipt of the notice, the tenant may not proceed under this section. label: hqa_Iowa_179
subject: Iowa
question: Does the law specify a minimum number of days a tenant can be late on rent before the landlord can file an eviction action for nonpayment?
final_answer:
According to the provided passages, specifically Source 1, section 648.3, and other related sections, the law in Iowa does specify procedures for eviction, including notice requirements for nonpayment of rent. However, the specific detail about the minimum number of days a tenant can be late on rent before the landlord can file an eviction action is not directly stated in the passages provided as being a part of Iowa law. The passages primarily discuss the methods of serving notice and the requirements for notices to quit, rather than the timing of when a landlord can initiate eviction proceedings based on late rent.

However, considering the context and typical legal frameworks, it's reasonable to infer that Iowa law, like many other jurisdictions, would have specific regulations regarding when a landlord can initiate eviction proceedings for nonpayment of rent. The passages provided do not explicitly state a minimum number of days for Iowa, but they do discuss notice periods and procedures, which imply that there are regulations governing the timing of eviction actions.

Given the information and focusing strictly on the question of whether the law specifies a minimum number of days a tenant can be late on rent before the landlord can file an eviction action, the answer would be based on the interpretation of the provided passages and the understanding of typical landlord-tenant laws.

Answer: No
snap_answer:
To determine if Iowa housing law specifies a minimum number of days a tenant can be late on rent before the landlord can file an eviction action for nonpayment, we need to consider the relevant statutes. In Iowa, the laws governing landlord-tenant relationships, including eviction procedures, are outlined in the Iowa Code. Specifically, Chapter 648 of the Iowa Code deals with forcible entry and detention, which includes the process for eviction.

According to Iowa Code §648.5, a landlord can initiate eviction proceedings against a tenant for nonpayment of rent. However, the statute requires that the landlord must first provide the tenant with a written notice stating the amount of rent due and demanding payment within a specified timeframe. This notice is often referred to as a "three-day notice" because it typically gives the tenant three days to pay the overdue rent or vacate the premises. If the tenant fails to comply with the notice by either paying the rent or moving out within the specified timeframe, the landlord can then proceed with filing an eviction action.

Given this information, it's clear that Iowa law does indeed specify a minimum timeframe (three days in the case of nonpayment of rent) that must elapse before a landlord can proceed with an eviction action for nonpayment of rent. This timeframe is part of the notice requirement that precedes the filing of an eviction lawsuit.

Answer: Yes

This statutory requirement effectively establishes a minimum timeframe that must expire before the landlord can file an eviction action. The notice period, as mandated by the statute, serves as a prerequisite to the eviction process, thereby specifying the minimum number of days a tenant can be late on rent.

A notice posted according to this paragraph shall be posted within the applicable time period for serving notice and shall include the date the notice was posted.   2. 2A:42-10.16a Three-day period for tenant to submit rent payment.  1. a. In an eviction action for nonpayment of rent, pursuant to subsection a. of section 2 of P.L.1974, c.49 (C.2A:18-61.1), the court shall provide a period of three business days after the date on which a warrant for removal is posted to the unit or a lockout is executed due to nonpayment of rent, for the tenant to submit a rent payment. A late fee shall not be imposed in excess of the amount set forth in the application for a warrant for removal if all rent due and owing is paid within the three business day period established by this subsection. b. (1) A landlord shall accept all payments of rent made by a tenant within the three business day period established by subsection a. of this section and upon payment of the rent due and owing, within two business days thereafter, the landlord shall provide the court with written notice that the rent due and owing was paid. A copy of this notice shall be provided to the tenant. (2) Upon receipt of the written notice as provided in this subsection, the court shall dismiss with prejudice the action for nonpayment of rent. (3) If the tenant makes a timely payment within the three business day period established by subsection a. of this section, and the landlord fails to provide the court with written notice of the rent payment, the tenant may file a motion to dismiss with prejudice the action for nonpayment of rent upon notice to the landlord. 799.65.  The management may terminate the tenancy of a defaulting tenant for nonpayment of rent, utilities, or reasonable incidental service charges, provided the amount due shall have been unpaid for a period of five days from its due date, and provided the tenant has been given a three-day written notice subsequent to that five-day period to pay the total amount due or to vacate the park. For purposes of this section, the five-day period does not include the date the payment is due. The three-day notice shall be given to the tenant in the manner prescribed by Section 1162 of the Code of Civil Procedure. Any payment of the total charges due, prior to the expiration of the three-day period, shall cure any default of the tenant. In the event the tenant does not pay prior to the expiration of the three-day notice period, the tenant shall remain liable for all payments due up until the time the tenancy is vacated.(Added by Stats. 1992, Ch. 310, Sec. 2. Effective January 1, 1993.)

label: hqa_Colorado_5365
subject: Colorado
question: Secondary methods of service are defined as those methods that may be used if the primary method is unsuccessful. Are posting and personal service to suitable person other than defendant and mail permitted secondary methods of service for an eviction action?
final_answer:
According to the provided passages, specifically Source 1, it is stated that "If personal service cannot be had upon the defendant by a person qualified under the Colorado rules of civil procedure to serve process, after having made diligent effort to make such personal service, such person may make service by posting a copy of the summons and the complaint in some conspicuous place upon the premises." Additionally, it mentions that the plaintiff shall mail a copy of the summons and the complaint to the defendant at the premises by postage prepaid, first-class mail. This indicates that posting and mail are indeed permitted secondary methods of service for an eviction action when personal service is unsuccessful.

Answer: Yes
snap_answer:
To determine if posting and personal service to a suitable person other than the defendant and mail are permitted secondary methods of service for an eviction action in Colorado, we need to consider the specific provisions of Colorado housing law, particularly those related to eviction proceedings and service of process.

1. **Understanding Primary and Secondary Service Methods**: In legal proceedings, including eviction actions, service of process is crucial. The primary method of service typically involves direct personal service to the defendant. If this primary method is unsuccessful, secondary methods may be employed.

2. **Colorado Law on Service of Process for Eviction**: Colorado law outlines specific procedures for serving eviction notices and lawsuits. For eviction actions, the law may allow for alternative or secondary methods of service if the primary method (usually personal service) cannot be achieved.

3. **Permitted Secondary Methods**: The question mentions posting, personal service to a suitable person other than the defendant, and mail as potential secondary methods. In many jurisdictions, including Colorado, these methods can be used under certain circumstances when primary service is not possible.

4. **Colorado Rules of Civil Procedure and Statutes**: The Colorado Rules of Civil Procedure (CRCP) and relevant statutes govern the service of process. According to these rules, if the primary method of service is unsuccessful, the plaintiff may use alternative methods, which can include leaving a copy of the summons and complaint at the defendant's usual abode (personal service to a suitable person), posting a copy at the defendant's usual abode, or using certified mail.

5. **Application to Eviction Actions**: For eviction actions specifically, Colorado law allows for these alternative methods under certain conditions, recognizing the challenges in serving defendants, especially in cases where they are avoiding service or have abandoned the premises.

Considering these steps and the specific context of Colorado housing law regarding eviction actions, it appears that posting, personal service to a suitable person other than the defendant, and mail can indeed be used as secondary methods of service if the primary method is unsuccessful.

Answer: Yes
hyde_passage: Pursuant to Rule 4(e) of the Colorado Rules of Civil Procedure, if the primary method of service is unsuccessful, a plaintiff may use alternative methods, which include leaving a copy of the summons and complaint at the defendant's usual abode with a suitable person (C.R.C.P. 4(e)(2)), posting a copy at the defendant's usual abode (C.R.C.P. 4(e)(3)), or using certified mail (C.R.C.P. 4(e)(1)). Additionally, Colorado Revised Statutes § 13-40-112(1) allows for service by mail and posting under certain circumstances for eviction actions.

evidence_store.text: Such summons may be served by personal service as in any civil action. A copy of the complaint must be served with the summons.    If personal service cannot be had upon the defendant by a person qualified under the Colorado rules of civil procedure to serve process, after having made diligent effort to make such personal service, such person may make service by posting a copy of the summons and the complaint in some conspicuous place upon the premises. In addition thereto, the plaintiff shall mail, no later than the next business day following the day on which he or she files the complaint, a copy of the summons, or, in the event that an alias summons is issued, a copy of the alias summons, and a copy of the complaint to the defendant at the premises by postage prepaid, first-class mail.    Personal service or service by posting shall be made at least seven days before the day for appearance specified in such summons, and the time and manner of such service shall be endorsed upon such summons by the person making service thereof.    For purposes of this section, “business days” means any calendar day excluding Saturdays, Sundays, and legal holidays.     History. Source: L. 1885: P. 227, § 11. L. 1887: P. 273, § 6. R.S. 08: § 2611. C.L. § 6377. CSA: C. 70, § 12. CRS 53: § 58-1-12. L. 61: P. 392, § 4. C.R.S. 1963: § 58-1-12. L. 64: P. 470, § 3. L. 83: (2) and (3) amended, p. 632, § 1, effective May 26. L. 84: (2) and (3) amended, p. 465, § 1, effective March 16. L. 87: (2) amended, p. 566, § 3, effective March 13. L. 2004: (2) and (3) amended and (4) added, p. 594, § 2, effective July 1. ANNOTATION   An actual appearance always cures a defective service of a summons, when, except for such defect, the court would have jurisdiction of the person and the subject matter of the suit. Tyler v. McKenzie, 43 Colo. 233 , 95 P. 943 (1908).   Landlord properly can take possession of abandoned apartment without resort to legal process. Martinez v. Steinbaum, 623 P.2d 49 (Colo. 1981).

Service of process upon any person subject to the jurisdiction of the courts of Colorado may be made by personally serving the summons upon the defendant or respondent outside this state, in the manner prescribed by the Colorado rules of civil procedure, with the same force and effect as if the summons had been personally served within this state.    No service of any summons or other process upon any corporation shall be made outside the state in the manner provided in subsection (1) of this section when such corporation maintains an agent for process upon whom service may be made as provided in rule 4 of the Colorado rules of civil procedure.    Nothing in this section shall limit or affect the right to serve any process as prescribed by the Colorado rules of civil procedure.     History. Source: L. 65: P. 472, § 2. C.R.S. 1963: § 37-1-27 . L. 82: P. 280, § 2.    Cross references:   For the manner of service, see C.R.C.P. 4.   ANNOTATION   Analysis     I. GENERAL CONSIDERATION.    II. PROPER SERVICE.     I. GENERAL CONSIDERATION.   Law reviews. For note, “One Year Review of Colorado Law -- 1964”, see 42 Den. L. Ctr. J. 140 (1965). For article, “Jurisdiction and Service of Process Beyond Colorado Boundaries”, see 11 Colo. Law. 748 (1982). For article, “Legislative Activities in Family Law”, see 11 Colo. Law. 1560 (1982).   This section and § 13-1-124 are sometimes referred to as the “long arm” or “single act” statute. Hoen v. District Court, 159 Colo. 451 , 412 P.2d 428 (1966); Cox v. District Court, 160 Colo. 437 , 417 P.2d 792 (1966).   Purpose of sections was to extend the court's jurisdiction. These sections were passed by the general assembly in order to extend, rather than to limit the jurisdiction of the courts of the state. White-Rodgers Co. v. District Court, 160 Colo. 491 , 418 P.2d 527 (1966).   Section may be used even when cause arose before effective date. This section and § 13-1-124 may be constitutionally applied where the complaint is filed after the effective date of the statute, though the tortious act complained of occurred before the effective date of the statute. Hoen v. District Court, 159 Colo. 451 , 412 P.2d 428 (1966); Cox v. District Court, 160 Colo. 437 , 417 P.2d 792 (1966).   Retrospective application of this section is in accord with sound public policy. Smith v. Putnam, 250 F. Supp. 1017 (D. Colo. 1965 ).   For discussion of conspiracy theory of personal jurisdiction, see Bennett Waites Corp. v. Piedmont Aviation, Inc., 563 F. Supp. 810 (D. Colo. 1983 ).   Applied in Nations Enters, Inc. v. Process Equip. Co., 40 Colo. App. 390, 579 P.2d 655 (1978); Adolph Coors Co. v. A. Genderson & Sons, 486 F. Supp. 131 (D. Colo. 1980 ); Beckman v. Carlson, 553 F. Supp. 1049 (D. Colo. 1983 ).   II. PROPER SERVICE.   Transacting of business may not be proved unless process is properly served. Since respondents did not serve process on a foreign corporation by personal service as required by the “long arm” statute, its provisions concerning contacts sufficient to establish doing business are inapplicable under § 7-9-119 . Geer Co. v. District Court, 172 Colo. 48 , 469 P.2d 734 (1970).   It is improper to dismiss a complaint because of improper or invalid service of process. Hoen v. District Court, 159 Colo. 451 , 412 P.2d 428 (1966).   Quashing of process is not reviewable by writ of error. An order quashing a purported service of process is not tantamount to a judgment of dismissal and under our rules is not such an order as is subject to review by writ of error. Hoen v. District Court, 159 Colo. 451 , 412 P.2d 428 (1966).   Process must be served on defendant at usual place of abode. The fact that the serviceman's usual place of abode was not the place of service is sufficient as a matter of law to overcome the prima facie showing made by the sheriff's return and that the service must therefore be set aside. Neher v. District Court, 161 Colo. 445 , 422 P.2d 627 (1967).   Term “usual place of abode” is not necessarily synonymous with domicile. The term “usual place of abode” has generally been construed to mean the place where that person is actually living at the time service is attempted. It is not necessarily synonymous with “domicile”. Neher v. District Court, 161 Colo. 445 , 422 P.2d 627 (1967).   For necessity of process being served by proper person, see Martin v. Denver Juvenile Court, 177 Colo. 261 , 493 P.2d 1093 (1972).   Process will not be quashed for lack of jurisdiction if plaintiff makes prima facie showing. Where plaintiff makes prima facie showing of threshold jurisdiction in complaint, process is not vulnerable to motion to quash based upon lack of jurisdiction. Texair Flyers, Inc. v. District Court, 180 Colo. 432 , 506 P.2d 367 (1973).

Any school located within or without this state that offers to provide educational services to any person in this state, whether the educational services are provided in person or by correspondence, or that offers to award any educational credentials to any person in this state submits the school to the jurisdiction of the courts of this state concerning any cause of action arising therefrom and for the purpose of enforcement of this article 64 by injunction pursuant to section 23-64-131 . Nothing contained in this section shall limit or affect the right to serve any process as prescribed by the Colorado rules of civil procedure.   History. label: hqa_Virginia_5952
subject: Virginia
question: Secondary methods of service are defined as those methods that may be used if the primary method is unsuccessful. Is personal service to suitable person other than defendant a permitted secondary method of service for an eviction action?
final_answer:
According to the provided passages, specifically Source 4, which pertains to the service of process in Virginia, it outlines various methods for serving natural persons. Section 2 of Source 4 describes "substituted service" as a method where, if the party to be served is not found at their usual place of abode, service can be made by delivering a copy of the process and giving information of its purport to any person found there who is a member of the party's family and is at least 16 years old. This can be considered a secondary method of service when the primary method (personal service to the defendant) is unsuccessful.

Given this context, personal service to a suitable person other than the defendant, such as a family member over 16 years old at the defendant's usual place of abode, is indeed a permitted secondary method of service for an eviction action, as it falls under the substituted service provision.

Answer: Yes
snap_answer:
To determine if personal service to a suitable person other than the defendant is a permitted secondary method of service for an eviction action in Virginia, we need to consider the relevant statutes and rules governing service of process in eviction cases.

1. **Understanding Primary and Secondary Methods of Service**: In legal proceedings, including eviction actions, service of process is crucial for notifying the defendant of the lawsuit. Primary methods are typically the first choice for serving defendants, and if these are unsuccessful, secondary methods may be employed.

2. **Virginia Law on Service of Process**: Virginia has specific laws governing how service of process must be carried out. For eviction actions, which are considered civil cases, the rules are outlined in the Virginia Code and the Virginia Rules of Court.

3. **Personal Service**: Personal service involves directly serving the defendant with the legal documents. If the defendant cannot be found or served personally, other methods may be used.

4. **Service to a Suitable Person**: In cases where personal service on the defendant is not possible, serving a suitable person can be an alternative. This might include an adult living at the defendant's residence or, in some contexts, an agent authorized to receive service on behalf of the defendant.

5. **Virginia's Specific Provisions**: According to Virginia law, for an eviction action (unlawful detainer), if personal service on the defendant is not possible, service can be made by leaving a copy of the summons at the defendant's usual abode in the presence of a member of the defendant's family who is above the age of 16 years and informing that person of the contents of the summons, or by delivering a copy of the summons to any person found on the premises who is above the age of 16 years and informing that person of the contents of the summons.

Given these considerations, personal service to a suitable person other than the defendant can indeed be considered a secondary method of service in the context of Virginia housing law for eviction actions, as it provides an alternative when direct personal service on the defendant is not feasible.

Answer: Yes
hyde_passage: Pursuant to § 8.01-296 of the Virginia Code, service of process in an eviction action may be effectuated by leaving a copy of the summons at the defendant's usual abode in the presence of a member of the defendant's family who is above the age of 16 years and informing that person of the contents of the summons, or by delivering a copy of the summons to any person found on the premises who is above the age of 16 years and informing that person of the contents of the summons, thereby permitting personal service to a suitable person other than the defendant as a secondary method. This provision outlines the specific circumstances under which service to a suitable person is deemed effective. As such, Virginia law explicitly allows for this method when personal service on the defendant cannot be achieved.

evidence_store.text: A. Subject to § 8.01-286.1, service of a process on a nonresident person outside the Commonwealth may be made by: (i) any person authorized to serve process in the jurisdiction where the party to be served is located; or (ii) any person 18 years of age or older who is not a party or otherwise interested in the subject matter of the controversy and notwithstanding any other provision of law to the contrary, such person need not be authorized by the circuit court to serve process which commences divorce or annulment actions. When the court can exercise jurisdiction over the nonresident pursuant to § 8.01-328.1, such service shall have the same effect as personal service on the nonresident within Virginia. The person so served shall be in default upon his failure to file a pleading in response to original process within 21 days after such service.
